agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to the precision of the delivery promise communicated to customers and used by operational teams. In logistics, the ETA is one of the most critical pieces of information — it drives customer behavior, operational planning, SLA enforcement, and contractual obligations.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Shipment Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)","definition":"The projected date and/or time a shipment will arrive at its destination. Currently expressed as an exact timestamp; proposed to become a date-only window."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is expected. An exact timestamp implies a zero-width window; a date-only value implies a full-day window (up to 24 hours of uncertainty)."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment shown to the end customer about when their package will arrive. This is a trust contract — overpromise erodes trust, underpromise loses competitiveness."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual commitment defining delivery performance targets, often measured against the communicated ETA. Changing ETA granularity directly affects how SLA compliance is measured."},{"term":"Last-Mile Delivery","definition":"The final leg of the delivery journey from distribution center to the recipient. This is where exact timestamps matter most for customer availability."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Confirmation that a shipment was delivered, typically timestamped. The gap between POD timestamp and communicated ETA determines perceived on-time performance."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the customer once a shipment is in transit.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customers and downstream systems depend on an ETA existing. A shipment in transit with no ETA is a broken tracking experience and violates basic logistics visibility requirements.","testable_assertion":"Every shipment with status IN_TRANSIT has a non-null ETA visible to the customer."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The ETA granularity shown to the customer must match the granularity used for SLA compliance measurement.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the customer sees 'by Friday' but the SLA is measured against '14:30 Friday', there is a fundamental disconnect — the customer could perceive on-time delivery while the SLA shows a miss, or vice versa. The two must be consistent to maintain operational integrity.","testable_assertion":"The unit of measurement for SLA compliance (date vs timestamp) matches the unit shown in the customer-facing ETA."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Changing ETA precision from timestamp to date-only expands the effective delivery window from minutes to up to 24 hours.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy decision about what level of precision to promise. A VP could decide to go back to timestamps or adopt hour-ranges.","testable_assertion":"When ETA format is date-only, the system treats any delivery on the stated date as on-time regardless of hour."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"ETA changes must not silently break existing API contracts with partners or integrated systems that consume timestamp-precision ETAs.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partner systems, webhook consumers, and downstream integrations may parse and depend on timestamp-precision fields. Removing time data without versioning the API corrupts their data pipelines.","testable_assertion":"Any API that previously returned a timestamp-precision ETA field continues to return timestamp precision, OR a versioned migration is provided with deprecation notice."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium/express service tiers may require timestamp-level ETA precision as part of their service definition.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Service tier definitions are policy decisions, but downgrading ETA precision for premium customers who pay for time-definite delivery would violate the value proposition of those tiers.","testable_assertion":"Shipments with service tier = EXPRESS or PREMIUM retain timestamp-precision ETAs even if standard shipments move to date-only."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment is in transit with a standard service tier","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only value (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no specific time shown."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A shipment is in transit with a premium or express service tier","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The ETA retains timestamp precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') because the service tier contractually promises time-definite delivery."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An external partner system consumes ETAs via the tracking API","when":"The API response is returned after the format change","then":"The API continues to return timestamp-precision ETAs in existing fields, OR a new versioned endpoint is provided, with a documented deprecation timeline for the old format."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A shipment is delivered at 23:59 on the ETA date","when":"SLA compliance is evaluated","then":"The delivery is counted as on-time because the promise was date-only and the delivery occurred on that date."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A shipment's ETA date changes (e.g., from Friday to Monday)","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The updated date is shown and a notification is sent explaining the delay, because date-level changes are now the smallest granularity of change visible to the customer."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The system stores internal operational ETAs","when":"Dispatchers and route planners view shipment details","then":"Internal systems retain timestamp-precision ETAs for operational planning regardless of what is shown to the customer."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Delivery scheduled for end-of-day but customer needs to be home to receive it","expected_behavior":"A date-only ETA gives the customer no ability to plan their availability. This is a major UX regression for deliveries requiring a signature or in-person receipt. The system should distinguish between drop-off-safe and signature-required deliveries."},{"scenario":"Same-day delivery service","expected_behavior":"Showing 'Delivery by Today' with no time is nearly useless for same-day service. Same-day shipments must retain time-level precision to have any meaningful ETA."},{"scenario":"Cross-timezone deliveries where sender and recipient are in different time zones","expected_behavior":"Date-only partially mitigates timezone display bugs (a minor benefit), but 'by Friday' must be interpreted in the recipient's local timezone, not UTC or the sender's timezone."},{"scenario":"ETA falls on a weekend or holiday when recipient location is a business address","expected_behavior":"'Delivery by Saturday' to a business closed on weekends is misleading. The system must account for recipient availability when showing date-only ETAs."},{"scenario":"Multiple packages in one order with different ETAs","expected_behavior":"If one package shows 'by Thursday' and another 'by Friday', the order-level ETA should show 'by Friday'. Date-only granularity makes split-shipment ETAs less confusing (minor benefit) but hides that one package arrives earlier."},{"scenario":"Historical reporting and analytics on delivery performance","expected_behavior":"Switching to date-only ETAs inflates on-time delivery metrics since the acceptable window is now 24 hours instead of minutes. Historical comparisons become invalid unless the metric definition change is explicitly tracked."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Mixed. Reduced precision lowers the bar for 'on-time' delivery, potentially reducing refund/credit claims from narrowly missed timestamp ETAs. However, premium customers who chose time-definite service may demand refunds or churn if precision is downgraded for their tier. If this change is applied uniformly, it devalues premium service tiers and could erode the price premium they command. Potential revenue risk from B2B partners whose SLAs reference timestamp-level commitments.","operational":"Positive in the short term — dispatchers face less pressure to hit exact times, reducing costly last-mile expediting. On-time delivery KPIs will mechanically improve (wider window = easier to hit), but this is a measurement artifact, not a real improvement. Internal operations MUST retain timestamp-level ETAs for route optimization, driver scheduling, and warehouse staging. This creates a dual-ETA system (internal timestamp, external date) that adds complexity.","user":"Significant regression for end consumers. Modern logistics (Amazon, FedEx, UPS) trend toward MORE precision (delivery windows of 1-2 hours), not less. Moving to date-only goes against industry direction and customer expectations. Customers who need to be present for delivery (signature required, high-value items, perishables) lose the ability to plan their day. Customer satisfaction scores for tracking experience will likely decrease.","risk":"HIGH. (1) API breaking change risk if partner integrations depend on timestamp fields. (2) Contractual risk if B2B SLAs reference time-level delivery commitments. (3) Competitive risk — customers may switch to carriers offering more precise ETAs. (4) Metric gaming risk — teams may use the inflated on-time rate to mask real delivery performance issues. (5) Regulatory risk in sectors with time-sensitive deliveries (pharmaceuticals, legal documents).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (B2C)","impact":"Lose ability to plan around delivery time. Increased missed deliveries for signature-required packages. Degraded tracking experience compared to competitors."},{"group":"Business Customers (B2B)","impact":"May face SLA contract misalignment. Warehouse receiving teams cannot schedule dock appointments without time precision. Potential contractual disputes."},{"group":"Partner Integrations / API Consumers","impact":"Breaking change if timestamp fields are removed or nulled. Data pipeline failures. Requires migration effort and timeline."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatchers","impact":"Short-term relief from exact-time pressure. But must maintain internal timestamp ETAs, creating dual-system complexity."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Increased inbound volume from customers asking 'what time will it arrive?' since the tracking page no longer answers this. Support cost increase."},{"group":"Product / Analytics Teams","impact":"Historical delivery performance metrics become incomparable across the change boundary. Must redefine KPIs and rebaseline."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"Harder to sell premium time-definite services if the platform signals a move away from time precision. Competitive positioning weakened."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal as stated is missing critical context needed for a confident analysis. The business rationale for the change is never stated — is it because ETAs are frequently inaccurate and causing complaints? Is it a technical simplification? A cost reduction? The answer fundamentally changes whether this is a good idea or a bad one. Additionally, we have no information about service tier definitions, existing SLA contracts, API consumer landscape, or current ETA accuracy rates — all of which are essential to evaluating impact.","high_confidence_areas":["The change expands the effective delivery window from minutes to ~24 hours","API consumers expecting timestamp fields will be affected","Internal operations must retain timestamp precision regardless","On-time delivery metrics will be artificially inflated","Industry trend is toward more precision, not less"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether premium/express service tiers exist and how they define ETA precision","Current ETA accuracy rate — if ETAs are wildly inaccurate today, date-only might actually improve trust","Existing B2B SLA contract language around delivery time commitments","Which API consumers exist and what fields they parse","Regulatory requirements for time-precision in specific verticals","The actual business motivation driving this proposal"],"assumptions":["Multiple service tiers exist (standard vs premium/express)","External API consumers exist that parse ETA timestamp fields","The change is proposed for customer-facing display, not internal operational systems","Current ETAs are timestamp-precise in both storage and display"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The business rationale for this change was never stated. Why move from timestamps to date-only? If current timestamp ETAs are inaccurate (e.g., <60% on-time to the hour), then date-only might actually IMPROVE customer trust by setting realistic expectations. If ETAs are accurate and this is purely a technical simplification, it is a clear regression. The recommendation changes completely based on this context.","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Product Team","Engineering"],"options":["Provide current ETA accuracy metrics (% on-time to the hour vs to the day)","State the business problem this change is intended to solve","Conduct customer research on ETA precision preferences"],"recommendation":"Before proceeding, the team must articulate WHY this change is being made. Share current ETA accuracy rates and the specific business problem being solved."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about existing service tier definitions and whether any tiers contractually guarantee time-definite delivery. Applying date-only ETAs to premium tiers could breach service commitments.","affected_stakeholders":["Business Customers","Sales","Legal"],"options":["Provide service tier definitions and their ETA precision guarantees","Confirm whether the change applies to all tiers or only standard"],"recommendation":"Enumerate all service tiers and confirm which ones have time-definite commitments before scoping this change."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information about the API consumer landscape. How many external systems consume timestamp-precision ETAs? What is the migration burden? This determines whether this is a minor display change or a major breaking platform change.","affected_stakeholders":["Partner Integrations","API Consumers","Engineering"],"options":["Audit API consumers and their field dependencies","Provide API documentation showing current ETA field schema"],"recommendation":"Conduct an API consumer audit before any implementation to quantify the migration scope."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information about existing B2B SLA contracts and whether they reference time-precision delivery commitments. Changing ETA granularity could put the company in breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["Business Customers","Legal","Account Management"],"options":["Legal review of B2B SLA language regarding delivery time commitments","Confirm whether SLA measurement will change alongside display"],"recommendation":"Legal must review all active B2B SLAs for time-precision language before this change ships."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to the precision of the delivery promise communicated to customers and used by operational teams. In logistics, the ETA is one of the most critical pieces of information — it drives customer behavior, operational planning, SLA enforcement, and contractual

content.thinking:
Analyzing the business impact of changing delivery ETAs from exact timestamps to date-only windows.

This is a domain analysis task about logistics ETA display changes. Let me think through this carefully.

Key observations:
- Current: exact timestamps ("Delivery by 14:30 on Friday")
- Proposed: date-only windows ("Delivery by Friday")
- This is a significant reduction in precision communicated to the customer

This is an ambiguity scenario - the proposal is vague. "Delivery by Friday" could mean end of business day, end of day, anytime Friday. Also, the business rationale isn't stated - are they doing this because ETAs are inaccurate? To reduce customer complaints about missed exact times? To simplify operations?


I'm missing critical context about why this change is being proposed, what the current accuracy rates look like, and how this impacts service level agreements or different customer segments. Since none of that information was provided, I'll work with what I have and produce the analysis.
